coverage_notes: Strong coverage of IOC consensus statements (2014, 2018, 2023), meta-analyses on LEA prevalence, male-specific and female-specific RED-S research, bone stress injury links, and elite distance runner populations. Minor gap: no RCT on RED-S intervention/treatment was found with verified DOI; most treatment evidence comes from consensus statements and narrative reviews.

papers.title: 2023 International Olympic Committee's (IOC) consensus statement on Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs)
papers.notes: The most current IOC consensus on REDs, updating the 2014 and 2018 statements. Introduces a new REDs conceptual model, the REDs Clinical Assessment Tool (CAT2), and a physiological model with primary and secondary indicators. Broadens RED-S beyond female athletes to all athletes and expands the health and performance consequences.

papers.title: IOC consensus statement on relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S): 2018 update
papers.notes: 2018 update to the original 2014 IOC RED-S consensus. Provides updated evidence on the health and performance effects of low energy availability across multiple body systems (bone, endocrine, metabolic, cardiovascular, immunological, psychological). Introduces the RED-S Clinical Assessment Tool (RED-S CAT) and Return to Play models.

papers.title: The IOC consensus statement: beyond the Female Athlete Triad--Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S)
papers.notes: The landmark paper that introduced the RED-S concept, expanding beyond the Female Athlete Triad to recognize that relative energy deficiency affects males and females across multiple physiological systems. Defined RED-S as impaired physiological function caused by relative energy deficiency including metabolic rate, menstrual function, bone health, immunity, protein synthesis, and cardiovascular health.

papers.title: Low Energy Availability and Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
papers.notes: Comprehensive meta-analysis of 59 studies including 6,118 athletes. Found 44.7% of athletes had low energy availability (44.2% female, 49.4% male). Athletes with LEA showed decreased run performance, training response, endurance performance, and increased illness absence. Most studies concluded LEA impairs bone health and increases bone stress injury risk.

papers.title: American College of Sports Medicine position stand. The female athlete triad
papers.journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
papers.notes: The foundational ACSM position stand defining the Female Athlete Triad as the interrelationship among energy availability, menstrual function, and bone mineral density. Each condition ranges on a spectrum from health to disease (eating disorders, amenorrhea, osteoporosis). Provided screening, diagnosis, prevention, and treatment guidelines that preceded the RED-S framework.

papers.title: 2014 Female Athlete Triad Coalition consensus statement on treatment and return to play of the female athlete triad: 1st International Conference held in San Francisco, CA, May 2012, and 2nd International Conference held in Indianapolis, IN, May 2013
papers.notes: Clinical consensus providing treatment and return-to-play guidelines for the Female Athlete Triad. Introduced a risk stratification point system for sport participation clearance decisions. Recommends multidisciplinary team management including physician, dietitian, and psychologist. Established clinical criteria for low-risk, moderate-risk, and high-risk categories.

papers.title: Low Energy Availability Is Difficult to Assess but Outcomes Have Large Impact on Bone Injury Rates in Elite Distance Athletes
papers.journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
papers.notes: Studied elite distance athletes, reporting energy availability, metabolic/reproductive function, bone mineral density, and injury/illness rates. Found that while LEA is difficult to quantify accurately, outcomes of LEA (menstrual dysfunction, low BMD) have large impact on bone stress injury rates. Compared diagnostic criteria from the Female Athlete Triad, LEAF-Q, and RED-S tools.

papers.title: Energy Availability in Athletics: Health, Performance, and Physique
papers.journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
papers.notes: Track-and-field-specific review of energy availability prevalence (18-58% in male and female athletes), with highest prevalence in endurance and jump events. Discusses LEA effects on testosterone, amenorrhea, bone health, and performance. Emphasizes early screening with tools like LEAF-Q and multidisciplinary treatment including sport physician, dietitian, physiologist, and psychologist.

papers.title: Low Energy Availability in Athletes 2020: An Updated Narrative Review of Prevalence, Risk, Within-Day Energy Balance, Knowledge, and Impact on Sports Performance
papers.notes: Comprehensive updated narrative review of LEA prevalence (22-58% across sports), risk factors, within-day energy deficiency patterns, athlete/coach knowledge gaps, and performance impacts. Highlights that within-day energy deficiency may be as important as overall daily energy availability, and that many athletes and coaches have poor knowledge of LEA/RED-S consequences.

papers.title: Relative energy deficiency in sports (RED-S): elucidation of endocrine changes affecting the health of males and females
papers.notes: Elucidates the sex-specific endocrine disruptions caused by RED-S. In females, energy deficiency suppresses GnRH pulsatility leading to hypoestrogenism and functional hypothalamic amenorrhea. In males, it reduces testosterone (Exercise-Hypogonadal Male Condition). Both pathways impair bone health. Highlights that male RED-S is under-recognized compared to females despite similar prevalence.
